---
bibliography: references.bib
bibliographystyle: apa
format:
  typst:
    template-partials:
      - typst-show.typ
    toc: false
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    margin:
      top: 30mm
      bottom: 20mm
      left: 40mm
      right: 25mm
    font-size: 11pt
    include-before-body:
      text: |
        // ==== TITLE PAGE — edit the fields marked with ← ====
        #page(numbering: none)[
          #v(2cm)
          #align(center)[
            #text(size: 20pt, smallcaps(strong[University of Hamburg]))
            #v(5mm)
            #text(size: 11pt)[
              Faculty of Business Administration \       // ← your faculty
              Institute for Logistics, Transport and Traffic // ← your institute
            ]
            #v(3cm)
            #text(size: 16pt, smallcaps(strong[Seminar Paper])) // ← Bachelor/Master/Seminar Paper
            #v(12mm)
            #text(size: 16pt, weight: "bold")[Title of the Thesis] // ← your title
            #v(12mm)
            #text(size: 11pt)[
              Independent academic work \
              for the degree of "Bachelor/Master of Science" \ // ← your degree
              in the program ...                               // ← your program
            ]
            #v(12mm)
            #text(size: 11pt)[Hamburg, #datetime.today().display()]
          ]
          #v(1fr)
          #grid(
            columns: (1fr, 1fr),
            column-gutter: 2em,
            [
              _submitted by:_ \
              #v(2mm)
              *Last Name, First Name* \    // ← your name
              #v(2mm)
              Matriculation No.: XXXXXXX \ // ← your matriculation number
              #v(2mm)
              Semester: X \                // ← your semester
              #v(2mm)
              E-Mail: first.last\@studium.uni-hamburg.de // ← your email
            ],
            [
              _supervised by:_ \
              #v(2mm)
              Prof. Dr. Knut Haase // ← your supervisor
            ],
          )
          #v(1cm)
        ]
        // ==== END TITLE PAGE ====
        #outline(depth: 3, indent: 1em)
        #pagebreak()
        #outline(title: "List of Figures", target: figure.where(kind: "quarto-float-fig"))
        #pagebreak()
        #outline(title: "List of Tables", target: figure.where(kind: "quarto-float-tbl"))
        #pagebreak()
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        #set par(leading: 0.85em)
        #show heading: it => { v(0.5em) + it + v(0.2em) }
        #show figure: it => { v(0.4em) + it + v(0.4em) }
        #set table(stroke: (_, y) => (
          top: if y == 0 { 0.08em } else if y == 1 { 0.05em },
        ))
        #show table: it => block(width: auto, stroke: (bottom: 0.08em), it)
        #show bibliography: it => {
          pagebreak()
          it
        }
---

# List of Abbreviations {.unnumbered}

<!-- Add or remove entries. Keep alphabetical order. Define each abbreviation on first use in text. -->

LLM : Large Language Model

OR : Operations Research

VRP : Vehicle Routing Problem

{{< pagebreak >}}

# List of Symbols {.unnumbered}

<!-- Add or remove entries. Keep alphabetical order. -->

$\mathcal{I}$ : Set of customers

$\mathcal{J}$ : Set of all locations (customers and depot)

$K$ : Number of vehicles

$c_{ij}$ : Travel cost from location $i$ to location $j$

$d_i$ : Demand of customer $i \in \mathcal{I}$

$Q$ : Vehicle capacity

$x_{ij}$ : Binary variable, 1 if a vehicle travels from $i$ to $j$

{{< pagebreak >}}

<!-- ============================================================
     HOW TO USE THIS TEMPLATE

     1. Edit the title page in the YAML block above (look for ← markers)
     2. Add your references to references.bib (use Zotero + Better BibTeX)
     3. Write your content below in Markdown
     4. Render: quarto render seminar-paper-en.qmd
     5. Live preview: quarto preview seminar-paper-en.qmd

     QUICK REFERENCE
     Citations:     [@key] → (Author, Year)    @key → Author (Year)
                    [@a; @b] → multiple        [@key, p. 42] → with page
     Cross-refs:    @fig-label  @tbl-label  @eq-label  @sec-label
     Figures:       ![Caption](path.png){#fig-label width="80%"}
     Tables:        pipe table + ": Caption {#tbl-label}" below
     Equations:     $$ ... $$ {#eq-label}
     Sections:      # Heading {#sec-label}
     ============================================================ -->

# Introduction {#sec-introduction}

<!-- Introduce the topic, motivate the research question, and outline the structure. -->

Operations research provides powerful tools for decision-making in complex systems. In recent years, advances in computational methods have enabled the solution of increasingly large-scale optimization problems [@desaulniers2005column]. This paper addresses the problem of ... .

@fig-example shows a schematic overview of the problem structure.

<!-- Figure syntax: ![Caption](images/file.png){#fig-label width="80%"} -->
![Replace this with your own figure](https://placehold.co/600x300/f0f0f0/333?text=Your+Figure+Here){#fig-example width="80%"}

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. @sec-literature reviews the relevant literature. @sec-model introduces the mathematical model, including the underlying assumptions in @sec-assumptions and the formal formulation in @sec-formulation. @sec-computational presents the computational study, and @sec-conclusion concludes with a summary and outlook for future research.



# Literature Review {#sec-literature}

<!-- Summarize relevant literature. Use [@key] and @key citations. -->

The vehicle routing problem has been studied extensively since the seminal work of @dantzig1959truck. Several exact and heuristic approaches have been proposed over the decades [@toth2014vehicle].


Column generation has proven to be a particularly effective technique for solving large-scale routing and scheduling problems [@desaulniers2005column]. Recent advances in machine learning have also been applied to improve heuristic performance [@bengio2021machine].

A comprehensive overview of solution methods is beyond the scope of this paper. For a detailed survey, the reader is referred to @toth2014vehicle.


# Model {#sec-model}

<!-- Overview, then split into assumptions and formulation. -->

This section introduces the mathematical model for the problem described above. We first state the key assumptions, then present the formal formulation.

## Assumptions {#sec-assumptions}

<!-- List the simplifying assumptions underlying your model. -->

The modeling of the problem requires several simplifying assumptions:

- A single depot serves as the starting and ending point for all vehicles.
- Each customer $i \in \mathcal{I}$ has a known demand $d_i$ that must be fully satisfied.
- The fleet consists of $K$ identical vehicles, each with capacity $Q$.
- Travel costs $c_{ij}$ between locations $i$ and $j$ are symmetric and satisfy the triangle inequality.

## Formulation {#sec-formulation}

<!-- Table caption: ": Caption {#tbl-label}" below the table.
     Equation label: {#eq-label} right after closing $$.
     Always explain the objective and each constraint in text. -->

We define the following notation:

| Symbol | Description |
|:------:|:------------|
| $\mathcal{I}$ | Set of customers |
| $\mathcal{J}$ | Set of all locations (customers and depot) |
| $K$ | Number of vehicles |
| $c_{ij}$ | Travel cost from location $i$ to location $j$ |
| $d_i$ | Demand of customer $i \in \mathcal{I}$ |
| $Q$ | Vehicle capacity |
| $x_{ij}$ | Binary variable, 1 if a vehicle travels from $i$ to $j$ |

: Notation overview {#tbl-notation}


The model is defined as follows:

$$
\min \sum_{i \in \mathcal{J}} \sum_{j \in \mathcal{J}} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$ {#eq-objective}

subject to:

$$
\sum_{j \in \mathcal{J}} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall i \in \mathcal{I}
$$ {#eq-assignment}

$$
\sum_{i \in \mathcal{J}} x_{ij} = 1 \quad \forall j \in \mathcal{I}
$$ {#eq-flow}

$$
x_{ij} \in \{0, 1\} \quad \forall i, j \in \mathcal{J}
$$ {#eq-binary}


The objective function (@eq-objective) minimizes total travel cost. @eq-assignment ensures that each customer is visited exactly once, while @eq-flow guarantees flow conservation. @eq-binary defines the binary decision variables. The notation is summarized in @tbl-notation.


# Computational Study {#sec-computational}

<!-- Describe setup, instances, and results. -->

To evaluate the performance of the proposed approach, we conduct a series of computational experiments. All experiments were performed on a machine with an Intel Core i7 processor and 16 GB of RAM. The model was implemented in Python using Gurobi 11.0 as the solver.

| Instance | $|\mathcal{I}|$ | Optimal | Heuristic | Gap (%) | Time (s) |
|----------|:---------------:|--------:|----------:|--------:|---------:|
| Small-1  |       25        |   245.3 |     248.1 |    1.14 |     0.8  |
| Small-2  |       25        |   312.7 |     318.4 |    1.82 |     1.1  |
| Medium-1 |       50        | 1,340.5 |   1,382.9 |    3.16 |    12.4  |
| Medium-2 |       50        | 1,128.3 |   1,167.2 |    3.44 |    15.7  |
| Large-1  |      100        | 3,450.1 |   3,621.3 |    4.96 |   245.3  |
| Large-2  |      100        | 4,217.8 |   4,485.6 |    6.35 |   310.2  |

: Computational results comparing exact and heuristic solutions {#tbl-results}

The results in @tbl-results show that the heuristic approach finds near-optimal solutions within reasonable computation times. For small instances, the optimality gap remains below 2%, while for larger instances the gap increases to approximately 5--6%.



# Conclusion and Outlook {#sec-conclusion}

<!-- Summarize contributions, then suggest future research directions. -->

This paper presented a mathematical model and heuristic solution approach for the vehicle routing problem. The computational study demonstrated that the proposed method achieves near-optimal solutions with significantly reduced computation times compared to exact methods.

Several directions for future research emerge from this work. First, the model could be extended to incorporate time windows and heterogeneous fleets. Second, the heuristic could be enhanced with machine learning techniques to improve initial solution quality. Finally, real-world case studies would help validate the practical applicability of the approach.


<!-- Bibliography is auto-generated from references.bib -->

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Declaration of Authorship {.unnumbered}

I hereby declare that I have written this thesis independently and without outside help, and that I have not used any sources or aids other than those indicated in the attached bibliography. All passages taken verbatim or in substance from published or unpublished sources are identified as such. All internet sources are attached to the thesis. Furthermore, I declare that I have not previously submitted this thesis in any other examination procedure and that the submitted written version corresponds to the version on the electronic storage medium.

```{=typst}
#v(2cm)
```

Hamburg, the \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

```{=typst}
#v(0.5cm)
```

First Name Last Name

{{< pagebreak >}}

# Use of AI Tools {.unnumbered}

<!-- List the AI tools you used and describe how you used them.
     Be specific: which tool, for which tasks, and how you verified the output. -->

<!-- List the AI tools you used below. Add or remove entries as needed.
     Be specific about what each tool was used for.
     Examples:
     - "ChatGPT: Used for brainstorming the structure of the literature review"
     - "Claude: Used for debugging Python code in the computational study"
     - "GitHub Copilot: Used for code completion in the implementation"
     - "Grammarly: Used for proofreading and improving clarity of Section 3"
     All AI-generated content must be reviewed, verified, and revised by you. -->

The following AI tools were used during the preparation of this thesis:

- **ChatGPT**: Used for ... .
- **Claude**: Used for ... .
- **GitHub Copilot**: Used for ... .

```{=typst}
#v(2cm)
```

Hamburg, the \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

```{=typst}
#v(0.5cm)
```

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

First Name Last Name
